title: How Large Language Models Work (Next-Word Prediction)
summary: This lecture explains the fundamental mechanism behind LLMs: next-word prediction. The model takes your input, predicts the most likely next word, adds it to the sequence, then predicts the next word, repeating until it reaches a stopping point. This was learned during training by showing the model vast amounts of internet text and having it predict masked words. The lecture emphasizes that LLMs use contextual knowledge — paying attention to relationships between words — to make predictions. It also covers key limitations: inherent randomness in outputs, knowledge cutoff dates, and the need to provide new information in prompts.

key_concepts.definition: The core mechanism of LLMs: given a sequence of text, predict the most likely next word/token, then repeat with the extended sequence.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: Understanding this mechanism helps you design prompts that set up the right context for the model to continue in the direction you want.

key_concepts.definition: The LLM's learned ability to pay attention to relationships between words in the input to make better predictions about what comes next.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: This is why 'Mary had a little' produces 'lamb' not 'bit' — the model uses context to disambiguate. Your prompt's context directly shapes the output.

key_concepts.definition: LLMs are trained on data collected up to a specific date and don't automatically update with new information.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: When you need current or private information, you must provide it in the prompt — the model can't access it otherwise.

key_concepts.definition: LLMs don't produce identical outputs for identical inputs — there's built-in variation in word selection during generation.
key_concepts.why_it_matters: Don't expect deterministic outputs. Build processes that account for variation, or use techniques to constrain it.

techniques: Think of your prompt as setting up context that the model will continue — structure it to lead toward your desired output.
Provide new or domain-specific information directly in the prompt since the model's knowledge has a cutoff date.
Expect variation in outputs — the same prompt won't produce identical results every time.
Don't expect perfection on the first try — plan for iteration and refinement.
Remember that the model learned patterns from internet text — it's good at continuing familiar patterns.

quiz_questions.question: What is the fundamental operation that large language models perform?
options: A. Searching a database for the best matching answer
B. Predicting the next word/token in a sequence, one at a time
C. Translating your question into code and executing it
D. Comparing your input against a fixed set of pre-written responses
quiz_questions.explanation: LLMs work by taking your input and predicting the next word, then adding that word to the sequence and predicting the next one, repeating until they reach a stopping point.

quiz_questions.question: Why does 'Mary had a little' produce 'lamb' instead of 'bit of peanut butter'?
options: A. Because 'lamb' is the most common word in English
B. Because the model uses contextual knowledge learned from training data where 'Mary had a little lamb' is a well-known nursery rhyme
C. Because the model has a database of nursery rhymes
D. Because 'lamb' always follows 'little' in English
quiz_questions.explanation: The model learned from vast training data that 'Mary had a little lamb' is a common pattern. It uses contextual relationships between words, not just word frequency, to make predictions.

quiz_questions.question: If you need the LLM to reason about information published after its training cutoff, what should you do?
options: A. Ask it to search the internet
B. Provide the new information directly in your prompt
C. Use a more recent model version
D. Both B and C could work, but B is always reliable
quiz_questions.explanation: The model's knowledge is frozen at its training cutoff. You can provide new information in the prompt, or use a newer model with a more recent cutoff — but even newer models have cutoffs, so providing information in the prompt is the most reliable approach.

raw_examples: Mary had a little → lamb, its fleece was white as snow...
Roses are red → violets are blue, sugar is sweet and so were you.
